title: Severe Hypothyroidism with an Incidental Positive ANA: When NOT to Chase Autoimmunity
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: TSH >100 as the real problem, the low-titre ANA (1:80 speckled) trap in Hashimoto, levothyroxine non-adherence vs malabsorption, who actually needs cortisol testing, B12/vitamin-D correction, and the right referral (endocrinology, not immunology)
authors: Resident/fellowship training module — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Hair fall and fatigue; markedly elevated TSH on routine testing.
pmh: Primary hypothyroidism on levothyroxine, taken intermittently ("off and on")

caseDescription: A 27-year-old woman (weight 50 kg) on levothyroxine 75 mcg taken 'off and on' presents with hair fall. TSH is >100 mIU/L. A panel sent at the same visit shows ANA positive, speckled pattern, reported 4+ at 1:80 dilution; vitamin B12 is 238 pg/mL (borderline-low) and 25-OH vitamin D is 16 ng/mL (deficient). She has no joint pain, no rash or skin lesions, no mouth ulcers, no Raynaud, no fever. The resident's plan: ignore the ANA as it is common in autoimmune thyroid disease, send a cortisol to screen for 'other autoimmune disease', and refer to an immunologist. The teaching question is whether that work-up is aimed at the right target.
keyFindings: TSH >100 mIU/L = severe, uncontrolled primary hypothyroidism — the dominant, treatable problem
Levothyroxine taken intermittently — non-adherence is the most likely reason TSH is so high on a weight-appropriate dose
ANA 1:80 speckled is a low-titre positive — non-specific, common in Hashimoto and in healthy people
No clinical features of systemic autoimmune disease (no arthritis, rash, serositis, Raynaud, cytopenias)
B12 238 pg/mL borderline-low and vitamin D 16 ng/mL deficient — correctable contributors to hair fall/fatigue
Hair fall (telogen effluvium) is explained by the hypothyroidism and the deficiencies, not by 'lupus'
finalDiagnosis: Severe primary hypothyroidism (almost certainly autoimmune/Hashimoto) that is uncontrolled because of levothyroxine non-adherence, with an incidental, clinically meaningless low-titre ANA and correctable B12/vitamin-D deficiency. Plan: restore daily levothyroxine with correct administration, recheck TSH/FT4 at 6–8 weeks, confirm Hashimoto with anti-TPO, and treat the deficiencies. The ANA needs no immunology referral and no further autoimmune hunt in the absence of symptoms; an early-morning cortisol/ACTH is reserved for clinical features of adrenal insufficiency, not for a positive ANA. The correct specialist, if any, is endocrinology.
learningObjectives: Recognise that TSH >100 on a weight-appropriate dose almost always means non-adherence/absorption, not under-dosing — and that this, not the ANA, is the priority.

Interpret a low-titre ANA (1:80) correctly: high pre-test sensitivity but low specificity; positive in Hashimoto and in ~5–25% of healthy people; not diagnostic of connective-tissue disease without clinical features.
Avoid the 'incidentaloma serology' trap — chasing a positive ANA with reflex referral/serology in an asymptomatic patient generates false leads, cost and anxiety.
Know the correct indications for cortisol/ACTH testing (clinical adrenal-insufficiency features, not a positive ANA) and the legitimate thyroid–adrenal links (Schmidt/APS-2).
Counsel on correct levothyroxine administration and the 6–8-week recheck interval, and correct B12/vitamin-D deficiency as part of the hair-fall work-up.

items.title: Why TSH >100 — not the ANA — is the headline of this case
items.content: Order the abnormalities by clinical weight, not by the order they were reported. A TSH above 100 mIU/L signals profound, uncontrolled primary hypothyroidism: this single number explains the fatigue, the hair fall (telogen effluvium), dry skin, menstrual disturbance and the patient's malaise. It is also entirely treatable. The positive ANA, by contrast, is an incidental, low-titre, non-specific finding in a patient with no symptoms of systemic autoimmune disease. The cognitive error to avoid is letting an exotic-looking serology hijack the consultation while the real, dangerous, fixable problem — uncontrolled hypothyroidism from missed tablets — is left unaddressed.
keyPoints: TSH >100 = severe primary hypothyroidism; this drives the symptoms
Hair fall here is hypothyroid telogen effluvium, not a lupus rash
Don't let an incidental ANA outrank the urgent, treatable problem

items.title: Why the first question with TSH >100 is adherence, not dose escalation
items.content: Full levothyroxine replacement is roughly 1.6 mcg/kg/day. At 50 kg that is about 80 mcg, so 75 mcg once daily is a sensible, near-replacement dose. A TSH above 100 on an adequate prescribed dose is therefore a red flag for the tablets not reaching the bloodstream — and by far the commonest reason is non-adherence ('off and on' is the clue the patient already gave). Reflexively pushing the dose higher in a non-adherent patient is unsafe: once she takes it consistently she may become iatrogenically thyrotoxic. The correct move is to fix adherence and administration first, then re-measure.
keyPoints: Replacement ≈ 1.6 mcg/kg/day → ~80 mcg at 50 kg; 75 mcg is appropriate
TSH >100 on an adequate dose ⇒ suspect non-adherence or malabsorption, not under-dosing
Don't escalate dose blindly in a non-adherent patient — risk of later thyrotoxicosis

items.title: How to take levothyroxine so it actually works
items.content: Levothyroxine absorption is fragile. Take it on an empty stomach with water and wait 30–60 minutes (ideally 60) before food, tea or coffee — coffee in particular blunts absorption. Separate it by ~4 hours from iron, calcium, multivitamins, antacids (PPIs/H2 blockers also reduce absorption), and from soy/high-fibre meals. A practical alternative for poor adherence is a single weekly supervised dose only if daily dosing keeps failing, but consistent daily dosing taken correctly is first-line. If TSH stays very high despite genuinely correct daily use, investigate true malabsorption (coeliac disease, atrophic gastritis, H. pylori) or pseudomalabsorption, and consider a supervised levothyroxine absorption test.
keyPoints: Empty stomach + water, wait 30–60 min before food/coffee
4-hour gap from iron, calcium, antacids, multivitamins; PPIs reduce absorption
Persistent TSH >100 despite correct use ⇒ work up malabsorption (coeliac, gastritis)

items.title: Why a 1:80 speckled ANA is a weak signal, not a diagnosis
items.content: ANA is a sensitive but non-specific screen. A titre of 1:80 is low — it is the conventional lower reporting cut-off and is found in roughly 5–25% of healthy people (more often in women and with age), and commonly in organ-specific autoimmune disease such as Hashimoto thyroiditis. The '4+' refers to fluorescence intensity at that low dilution, not to disease severity. A speckled pattern is the least specific pattern. Crucially, ANA only becomes clinically meaningful in the presence of compatible clinical features (arthritis, photosensitive rash, serositis, Raynaud, cytopenias, renal disease). In an asymptomatic patient, a low-titre ANA has a very low positive predictive value for connective-tissue disease — testing it here was itself the misstep.
keyPoints: 1:80 = low titre; present in 5–25% of healthy people and in Hashimoto
'4+' = fluorescence intensity, not disease severity; speckled = least specific pattern
ANA only matters alongside clinical features — pre-test probability drives interpretation

items.title: How to decide whether a positive ANA needs chasing
items.content: Anchor on pre-test probability. Take a focused review of systems for connective-tissue disease: inflammatory joint pain/swelling, photosensitive or malar rash, oral/nasal ulcers, alopecia areata-type patches, Raynaud, dry eyes/mouth, pleuritic chest pain, unexplained fever, and look at the basic labs (CBC for cytopenias, urinalysis for proteinuria/haematuria, creatinine). If all are negative, a low-titre ANA is observed, not worked up — no reflex ENA/dsDNA, no complement, no immunology referral. Only if specific features are present do you order the targeted next tests (e.g. anti-dsDNA and complement for suspected SLE). Chasing ENA panels off a lone 1:80 ANA generates false-positive distractions, cost and patient anxiety.
keyPoints: Screen ROS + basic labs (CBC, urinalysis, creatinine) for CTD features
All negative ⇒ observe the ANA; do not reflex ENA/dsDNA/complement
Targeted serology only when specific clinical features appear

items.title: Why 'send to immunologist' is the wrong referral here
items.content: The resident's instinct to 'ignore the ANA' is correct, but the rest of the plan contradicts it. Referring an asymptomatic patient to an immunologist/rheumatologist purely for a 1:80 ANA is exactly the chasing they said they wanted to avoid — it medicalises a normal-range finding and outsources reassurance. The patient does not need an immunologist; she needs her hypothyroidism controlled. If any specialist is involved, it is endocrinology, for the severe, poorly controlled hypothyroidism and adherence support — and even that is often manageable in primary care. Referral to rheumatology becomes appropriate only if genuine connective-tissue features emerge.
keyPoints: Referring for a lone 1:80 ANA = the chasing you meant to avoid
Right specialist (if any) = endocrinology for the uncontrolled thyroid, not immunology
Rheumatology referral only if true CTD features develop

items.title: Why a cortisol test is not indicated 'to screen for other autoimmunity'
items.content: A positive ANA is not an indication for cortisol testing, and 'checking cortisol for other autoimmune disease' confuses two different things. The legitimate thyroid–adrenal link is autoimmune polyglandular syndrome type 2 (Schmidt syndrome): autoimmune (Addison) adrenal insufficiency clustering with autoimmune thyroid disease and type 1 diabetes. But you test for adrenal insufficiency when there is a clinical reason to — postural hypotension, unexplained weight loss, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, recurrent vomiting/diarrhoea, hyponatraemia/hyperkalaemia, profound fatigue — not because an ANA came back positive. The correct screen, when indicated, is an 8 a.m. cortisol (± ACTH), proceeding to a short Synacthen test if low/borderline. One genuine caveat: in coexisting untreated hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency, starting thyroxine before steroids can precipitate adrenal crisis — so if adrenal insufficiency is clinically suspected, evaluate/cover it first.
keyPoints: ANA positivity is NOT an indication to check cortisol
Test for Addison only with clinical features (hypotension, hyperpigmentation, low Na/high K, weight loss)
APS-2/Schmidt is the real link; if AI is suspected, treat steroids before thyroxine to avoid crisis

items.title: How to confirm this is Hashimoto — and why anti-TPO, not ANA, is the test
items.content: The serology that actually matters for this patient is anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibody, which confirms autoimmune (Hashimoto) thyroiditis as the cause of her primary hypothyroidism. (Anti-thyroglobulin adds little.) A positive anti-TPO also predicts persistence and the need for lifelong replacement, and informs counselling around pregnancy. It is a far more relevant 'autoimmune' test here than the ANA. Note this is the likely reason the ANA is positive at all: organ-specific autoimmune disease frequently produces low-titre, non-specific ANA positivity without any systemic autoimmune disease.
keyPoints: Anti-TPO confirms Hashimoto as the cause — the relevant autoantibody here
Hashimoto itself commonly causes a low-titre, non-specific ANA
Positive anti-TPO ⇒ lifelong replacement; matters for pregnancy counselling

items.title: Why the hair fall is multifactorial — and what to correct
items.content: Diffuse hair shedding (telogen effluvium) in this patient has at least three correctable drivers, all of which point away from lupus: (1) the hypothyroidism itself — restoring euthyroidism is the single biggest lever; (2) vitamin D deficiency at 16 ng/mL (deficient; aim to replace toward ≥30 ng/mL); and (3) borderline-low B12 at 238 pg/mL, which can also cause fatigue and, if it falls further, neurological symptoms. Iron deficiency is the other classic culprit, so a CBC and ferritin/iron studies belong in the same work-up. Hair recovery lags biochemical correction by weeks to a few months, which is worth saying to the patient so she persists with treatment rather than abandoning it.
keyPoints: Treat the hypothyroidism first — biggest single driver of the hair fall
Replace vitamin D (16 = deficient) and address borderline B12 (238)
Add CBC + ferritin/iron studies; warn that hair regrowth lags correction by weeks–months

items.title: Why borderline B12 in autoimmune thyroid disease deserves a second look
items.content: A B12 of 238 pg/mL sits in the borderline-low zone where the serum assay is unreliable. Autoimmune thyroid disease clusters with autoimmune (pernicious) gastritis, so the threshold to investigate should be low. If symptoms suggest deficiency (fatigue, glossitis, paraesthesiae) or there is macrocytosis on the CBC, confirm with metabolic markers — methylmalonic acid (the more specific) and homocysteine, both rise in true deficiency — and consider anti-intrinsic-factor/parietal-cell antibodies if pernicious anaemia is suspected. Replace if confirmed or strongly suspected; don't anchor on a single borderline number.
keyPoints: B12 238 is borderline — serum assay unreliable in this range
Confirm with methylmalonic acid (specific) ± homocysteine if symptomatic/macrocytic
Low threshold for pernicious-anaemia work-up given autoimmune thyroid clustering

items.title: How and when to recheck after restarting levothyroxine
items.content: TSH responds slowly. After re-establishing consistent, correctly-taken daily levothyroxine, recheck TSH and free T4 at 6–8 weeks — earlier testing reflects the old state and tempts premature dose changes. Titrate to bring TSH into the target range (broadly 0.4–4.0 mIU/L for a non-pregnant adult; lower-half targeting if pregnancy is planned). If TSH remains grossly elevated at 6–8 weeks despite verified correct administration, that is the trigger to investigate malabsorption and drug interactions rather than simply piling on dose. Once stable, annual TSH suffices.
keyPoints: Recheck TSH + FT4 at 6–8 weeks — not sooner
Target TSH ~0.4–4.0 (lower half if planning pregnancy)
Persistent gross elevation despite correct use ⇒ malabsorption work-up, not blind dose-piling

items.title: How to handle an incidental positive ANA — a one-page rule
items.content: Practical algorithm for any incidental positive ANA: (1) Ask why it was ordered — ANA should be tested when SLE/CTD is clinically suspected, not as a routine 'autoimmune screen'. (2) Note the titre and pattern — 1:40–1:80 is low and weakly significant; ≥1:320 with a relevant pattern raises concern. (3) Screen for CTD features (joints, skin, serositis, Raynaud, sicca) and basic labs (CBC, urinalysis, creatinine). (4) Features absent ⇒ observe, reassure, no reflex serology, no referral; document. (5) Features present ⇒ targeted next tests (dsDNA, ENA, complement) and rheumatology referral. The teaching point: the test result is interpreted through the patient, not the other way round.
keyPoints: Order ANA only when CTD is clinically suspected, not as a routine screen
Low titre + no features ⇒ observe, reassure, document; no referral
Features present ⇒ targeted serology + rheumatology referral

items.question: Why can a positive ANA be safely 'observed' in this patient but would demand action in another?
items.answer: Because the meaning of a positive ANA is determined by pre-test probability, not by the result alone. ANA is highly sensitive but poorly specific; a low titre (1:80, speckled) is common in healthy people and in organ-specific autoimmunity such as Hashimoto. In this woman there are no connective-tissue-disease features and presumably normal basic labs, so the positive predictive value for SLE/CTD is very low and the result is observed. In a different patient — say one with inflammatory polyarthritis, a photosensitive malar rash, oral ulcers and proteinuria — the same titre carries a high post-test probability of lupus and mandates targeted serology (dsDNA, complement) and rheumatology input. Same number, opposite action, because the clinical context changes the probability.
keyPoints: ANA significance = pre-test probability × titre/pattern, not the result alone
No CTD features ⇒ low PPV ⇒ observe
CTD features present ⇒ high PPV ⇒ targeted serology + referral

items.question: How should the work-up actually be sequenced for this patient?
items.answer: Lead with the thyroid. (1) Restore consistent daily levothyroxine 75 mcg taken correctly (empty stomach, 30–60 min before food/coffee, 4 h from iron/calcium/antacids) and counsel on adherence. (2) Confirm the cause with anti-TPO. (3) Correct the deficiencies — replace vitamin D (16 = deficient), evaluate and treat borderline B12 (238) including pernicious-anaemia work-up if indicated, and add CBC + ferritin/iron studies for the hair fall. (4) Recheck TSH and free T4 at 6–8 weeks and titrate. (5) For the ANA: take a CTD review of systems and check basic labs; if negative, observe and reassure — no ENA/dsDNA, no immunology referral. (6) Reserve an 8 a.m. cortisol (± Synacthen) for genuine clinical suspicion of adrenal insufficiency, not for the ANA. The ANA and cortisol are the lowest priorities, not the first moves.
keyPoints: Thyroid first: restart correct daily LT4 + adherence counselling
Confirm with anti-TPO; correct vitamin D/B12/iron for the hair fall
Recheck TSH/FT4 at 6–8 weeks; observe the ANA; cortisol only if AI suspected

items.stem: A 27-year-old woman (50 kg) on levothyroxine 75 mcg taken 'off and on' has TSH >100 mIU/L, ANA 1:80 speckled, B12 238 pg/mL, vitamin D 16 ng/mL and hair fall, with no joint or skin symptoms. What is the single most important next step?
options: Re-establish consistent, correctly-taken daily levothyroxine and counsel on adherence
Refer to an immunologist for the positive ANA
Send anti-dsDNA, ENA panel and complement levels
Start oral prednisolone for presumed autoimmune disease
items.explanation: The dominant, dangerous, treatable problem is uncontrolled hypothyroidism (TSH >100), almost certainly from non-adherence on an otherwise appropriate dose. Fixing adherence and administration is the priority. The 1:80 ANA is incidental with no CTD features, so reflex serology, immunology referral and empirical steroids are all inappropriate.

items.stem: Levothyroxine replacement is about 1.6 mcg/kg/day. At 50 kg, with the patient admitting intermittent use, how should you interpret a TSH >100 on 75 mcg?
options: The prescribed dose is appropriate; the high TSH most likely reflects non-adherence or malabsorption
The dose is grossly inadequate and should be doubled immediately
The patient is over-replaced and the dose should be reduced
The TSH assay is unreliable and should be ignored
items.explanation: 75 mcg ≈ a near-full replacement dose for 50 kg (~80 mcg). A TSH >100 on an adequate prescribed dose, in a patient who takes it 'off and on', points to tablets not being absorbed — non-adherence first, malabsorption second. Blindly doubling the dose risks thyrotoxicosis once she takes it consistently.

items.stem: How is the ANA result of '1:80, speckled, 4+' best interpreted in this asymptomatic patient?
options: A low-titre, non-specific positive that is common in Hashimoto and healthy people; observe
A strongly positive result diagnostic of systemic lupus erythematosus
Evidence of active connective-tissue disease requiring immediate treatment
An assay error that should simply be repeated until negative
items.explanation: 1:80 is a low titre; '4+' describes fluorescence intensity, not disease severity, and speckled is the least specific pattern. Low-titre ANA is found in 5–25% of healthy people and commonly in Hashimoto. With no CTD features it is observed, not treated. It is neither diagnostic of SLE nor, on its own, an assay error.

items.stem: The resident wants to 'check cortisol to screen for other autoimmune disease' because the ANA is positive. Which statement is correct?
options: Cortisol/ACTH testing is indicated only for clinical features of adrenal insufficiency, not for a positive ANA
A positive ANA is a recognised indication to measure cortisol
Every patient with autoimmune thyroid disease needs routine cortisol screening
A random afternoon cortisol reliably excludes Addison disease
items.explanation: ANA positivity does not indicate cortisol testing. Screen for adrenal insufficiency only with clinical features (postural hypotension, weight loss, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia/hyperkalaemia) — the APS-2/Schmidt link is real but symptom-driven. The correct screen is an 8 a.m. cortisol ± Synacthen, not a random afternoon level.

items.stem: Which single test best confirms the cause of this woman's primary hypothyroidism?
options: Anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibody
Antinuclear antibody (ANA) titre
Thyroid ultrasound with elastography
Serum thyroglobulin level
items.explanation: Anti-TPO confirms autoimmune (Hashimoto) thyroiditis, the overwhelmingly likely cause, and predicts persistence/lifelong replacement. ANA is non-specific and not a thyroid test; ultrasound and thyroglobulin do not establish autoimmune aetiology here.

items.stem: Which administration instruction is most likely to improve her levothyroxine absorption?
options: Take it on an empty stomach with water and wait 30–60 minutes before food or coffee
Take it with breakfast and morning coffee for consistency
Take it together with her calcium and iron supplements
Take it with an antacid to protect the stomach
items.explanation: Levothyroxine should be taken fasting with water, 30–60 minutes before food or coffee (coffee blunts absorption), and separated by ~4 hours from calcium, iron and antacids, all of which impair absorption. The other options each reduce absorption.

items.stem: After she restarts consistent, correctly-taken levothyroxine, when should TSH and free T4 be rechecked?
options: At 6–8 weeks
At 3–5 days
At 2 weeks
Only after 6 months
items.explanation: TSH equilibrates slowly; 6–8 weeks is the standard interval after a dose change or restart. Earlier testing reflects the prior state and tempts premature, unnecessary dose changes.

items.stem: Which combination best explains and addresses her diffuse hair fall?
options: Hypothyroidism plus vitamin D deficiency and borderline B12, with iron studies to complete the work-up
Systemic lupus erythematosus driven by the positive ANA
Androgenetic alopecia requiring finasteride
Adrenal insufficiency requiring hydrocortisone
items.explanation: Diffuse shedding (telogen effluvium) here is multifactorial: uncontrolled hypothyroidism, vitamin D deficiency (16) and borderline B12 (238), with iron deficiency the other classic cause to exclude (CBC, ferritin). The ANA does not establish lupus, and there is no basis for finasteride or steroids.

items.statement: A positive ANA at 1:80 in a patient with no symptoms or signs of connective-tissue disease usually warrants reflex anti-dsDNA, ENA and complement testing plus rheumatology referral.
items.explanation: False. With no CTD features, a low-titre ANA has very low positive predictive value and should be observed, not chased. Reflex serology and referral are reserved for patients with compatible clinical features.

items.statement: A TSH above 100 mIU/L on a weight-appropriate levothyroxine dose should prompt consideration of non-adherence or malabsorption before simply escalating the dose.
items.explanation: True. On an adequate dose, a grossly elevated TSH points to the drug not being absorbed — non-adherence first, then malabsorption (coeliac, atrophic gastritis) or interacting drugs. Blind dose escalation risks later thyrotoxicosis once intake becomes consistent.

items.assertion: This asymptomatic patient should have a cortisol level checked because her ANA is positive.
items.reason: A positive ANA is an established indication for screening for adrenal insufficiency.
items.explanation: Both statements are false. A positive ANA is not an indication for cortisol testing, and it is not an established screen for adrenal insufficiency. Cortisol/ACTH testing is driven by clinical features of Addison disease (hypotension, weight loss, salt craving, hyperpigmentation, hyponatraemia/hyperkalaemia), not by serology. Hence option 4.

items.assertion: If this patient genuinely had coexisting untreated adrenal insufficiency, levothyroxine should be started only after glucocorticoid cover.
items.reason: Starting thyroxine before correcting adrenal insufficiency can increase cortisol clearance and metabolic demand and precipitate an adrenal crisis.
items.explanation: Both statements are true and the reason explains the assertion. In coexisting untreated hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency (as in APS-2/Schmidt), thyroxine raises metabolic rate and cortisol turnover; giving it before steroid replacement can precipitate an adrenal crisis — so glucocorticoids are started first. Hence option 0. (Note: this sequencing matters only if adrenal insufficiency is actually suspected — it is not a reason to test cortisol off a positive ANA.)

items.question: Give two reasons a 1:80 speckled ANA is a weak signal in this patient.
items.answer: (1) It is a low titre — 1:80 is the conventional lower cut-off and is found in 5–25% of healthy people and commonly in Hashimoto thyroiditis. (2) She has no connective-tissue-disease features (no arthritis, rash, serositis, Raynaud, cytopenias), so the pre-test probability — and therefore the positive predictive value — for SLE/CTD is very low. The speckled pattern is also the least specific pattern, and '4+' denotes fluorescence intensity, not disease severity.

items.question: What clinical features would actually justify checking an 8 a.m. cortisol in a patient with autoimmune thyroid disease?
items.answer: Features of adrenal insufficiency: postural hypotension or fainting, unexplained weight loss, salt craving, hyperpigmentation (especially of palmar creases, buccal mucosa, scars), recurrent vomiting/diarrhoea, profound weakness, and biochemical clues such as hyponatraemia with hyperkalaemia. These suggest possible Addison disease within an autoimmune polyglandular syndrome (APS-2/Schmidt). A positive ANA is not one of these indications.

items.question: Summarise, in priority order, the correct plan for this patient.
items.answer: (1) Restore consistent daily levothyroxine 75 mcg taken correctly (empty stomach, 30–60 min before food/coffee, 4 h from iron/calcium/antacids) with adherence counselling. (2) Confirm Hashimoto with anti-TPO. (3) Correct deficiencies — replace vitamin D (16), evaluate/treat borderline B12 (238) with pernicious-anaemia work-up if indicated, add CBC + ferritin/iron studies for the hair fall. (4) Recheck TSH and free T4 at 6–8 weeks and titrate. (5) Observe and reassure on the ANA — no reflex serology, no immunology referral, given no CTD features. (6) Reserve an 8 a.m. cortisol (± Synacthen) for genuine adrenal-insufficiency suspicion. The right specialist, if any, is endocrinology — not immunology.
